reasoning: The plural is biri and interrogative is bi-bo, indicating the root is bi. Singular has zero suffix, plural adds -ri. Pattern from similar short roots like go (zero singular, -ri plural).
Singular bong and plural bonni suggest root bon (ng assimilates in plural to nni). Interrogative adds -bo to root, consistent with all other interrogatives ending in -bo.
Singular bono similar to bong (lizard class with back vowel o/ng). Plural follows -nni pattern like lizard bonni. Interrogative bo-no likely transcription error for bon-bo, fitting -bo pattern.
Singular dere and plural derr indicate root der (singular +e, plural double r). Interrogative adds -bo to root, dropping singular -e, consistent with mountain tan-bo.
Interrogative tolofu-bo indicates root tolofu. Matches maggot class: singular adds -i to root (-uli), plural -o (-ulo).
Plural form in -ulo class, adding -o to root tolofu, parallel to dundulo from dunduli.
Interrogative ti-bo indicates root ti. Fits class B: singular root + -ri, like sebiri, nimiri.
Plural for class B: root + -e, like sebie, nimie from roots sebi, nimi.
Interrogative nti-bo indicates root nti. Fits class B: singular root + -ri.
Plural for class B: root nti + -e.
Interrogative nmar-bo indicates root nmar (with a vowel). Fits class C: singular root + -r, like nyagr.
Plural for class C: root nmar + -a, like nyaga.
Interrogative po-bo indicates root po. Fits class A: singular zero suffix, like go, pi; plural por = po + r(i).
Interrogative tan-bo indicates root tan. Fits class E: singular root + -e, like dere; plural doubles final n to tann.
Interrogative weg-bo indicates root weg. Fits class A: singular zero, plural + -ri = wegri, like go - gori.
Interrogative wul-bo indicates root wul. Fits drum class: singular zero (consonant-final), like il.
Interrogative yag-bo indicates root yag (a vowel). Fits class C: singular root + -r, like nyagr; plural yaga.

answer: Dagare nouns are inflected for number (singular/plural) and have an interrogative form used for questions, typically derived by adding -bo to the noun root. Nouns fall into multiple classes with distinct affixation patterns, likely governed by vowel harmony (advanced vs. unadvanced tongue root vowels) and phonological features like final consonants. Key patterns include: 1. Class with singular -ri, plural -e (e.g., sebiri/sebie, nimiri/nimie, tiri/tie, ntiri/ntie). 2. Class with singular zero, plural -ri (e.g., go/gori, pi/pir, weg/wegri, po/por). 3. Class with singular -r, plural -a (e.g., nyagr/nyaga, yagr/yaga, nmarr/nmara). 4. Class with singular -uli, plural -ulo (e.g., dunduli/dundulo, tolofui/tolofuo). 5. Class with singular -e, plural doubled final consonant (e.g., dere/derr, tane/tann). 6. Unmarked class (singular = plural, e.g., kpankpaniele). 7. Class changing -ogo to -ogr in plural (e.g., nimisogo/nimisogr). 8. Class adding -ri to -o ending singular (e.g., valenyo/valenyori). 9. Class with singular -i, plural -ie (e.g., gbebi/gbebie). 10. Special class for certain animals: plural -nni, from singular ending in -ong or -ono (e.g., bong/bonni, bono/bonni). Interrogatives consistently append -bo to the root, omitting class-specific singular suffixes.
